to be clear to those not in the know, jalan alor here is a nonmuslim, chinese-centered streetfood night market, hence offerings native to chinese cuisine like stinky tofu and frogs, as well as alcohol and pork can be found. muslim-centered streetfood markets of course, offers halal foods only. regardless of which though the food served is not monotone to the ethnic/religion; not uncommon to also find indian, thai, indonesian and even western cuisine among the selections.and unlike common western stereotypes on asian, malaysian chinese(and singaporean too) is not as.. extreme as those in the mainland so you won't find crazy stuffs like insects, snakes, bats or worse, pets being served.

welcome to malaysia! glad to see you and tyler enjoyed our street food so much!just a bit of info on those colorful dimsum— the goth meatball is wrapped in seaweed, which gives it the black color. not sure about the sakura one, we don’t eat that as it’s not part of the 'og' dimsum family lol. the yellow one is called siu mai, and the good ones are usually made with pork and prawn with fish roe on top. if you ever get the chance to visit a proper dimsum restaurant, you’ll be blown away!

at 43:10, the dish is not a roti, chapati, or paratha; the correct name is 'parotta.' there are various types of parotta, and the one featured in the video is a fried parotta, partially fried in a substantial amount of oil or ghee. it originated in the tamil-populated jaffna region of sri lanka and was later introduced in tamil nadu. parotta is a renowned culinary delight in southern india. it's important to note that many people often mistake it for 'paratha,' a famous north indian dish."

muar otak2 comes from muar, a town on the west coast about a third of the way from johor baru to melaka. muar is famous in malaysia for having the best spoken malay language in the country and is the basis for the official malay language bahasa malaysia (or bahasa melayu, as it was when i was a kid.)you paid myr 66 for that satay, and they didn't give you any ketupat/nasi impit? get a bread roll from the hotel's breakfast buffet tomorrow and soak up the rest of that sauce. or just be a pig like me and drink it straight out of the bag!nasi lemak has evolved from a bare bones meal that the poorer labourers would eat on their way to work to fanciful constructions with more exotic ingredients and more of them.that sirap mawar (rose syrup) is also called sirup bandung in indonesia.

first one is otak otak. yup, i would recommend village park if people are willing to travel further. wanjo if they wanna stick to kl.try these on your next kl visit. pantai district:lemang pantai power - lemang is glutinous rice with coconut milk cooked in a bamboo. usually served with rendang or serunding (meat floss).kak nik patin house - this one serves tempoyak (fermented durian). pais patin is grilled catfish with sambal tempoyak. gulai patin tempoyak is another (lighter and a lot more sauce).nasi kerabu moknab - nasi kerabu is salad rice. comes with grilled meat and malay dishes.petaling street area:sin kiew yee shin kee beef noodles - beef noodles (soup version)soong kee beef noodles & low yau kee porridge - beef noodles (dry version)lai foong lala noodles - lala is clam.ah har corner - hakka noodlesbukit bintang / imbi area:mon chinese beef rotiah hei bak kut teh - chinese herbal soup with pork varietiesbest hokkien mee (kl version) goes to petaling street charcoal hokkien mee at jinjang. search for kedai kopi seng wai if the directions is weird.

the problem of malaysian street food, every shop have slight different taste, even though it's the same dish.so having the same dish from a different shop/stall will have different taste that depending on how the chef/cook prepare the dish.so, as a local malaysian, we roam around the town/country just to try different shop's dishes to find the best one that fits our palates for our entire life. xdalso, blood osyters are very common topping on chinese style char koey teow.and chee cheong fan is actually a chinese dish, only reason why it was prep by malay is that it can easily be prep halal, caz the dish does not uses pork nor any ingredients that considered non-halal. it is a very common dish you can definitely find in any chinese morning market, or alot of chinese kopitiam in malaysia.

sir, let me explain about the hainanese western food.. it is not the case of "somebody watched a lot of hollywood movies which gives the ideas what the westerners eat".. the malaysian western food has its own origin way back during the colonial era in the late 1800 until our independence in 1950'sduring that time a lot of hainanese in malaya worked as kitchen helper for the british officers or european planters or european tin miners.the brits and other europeans taught all this hainanese kitchen helper to cook some food that mimics the europeans food by using local ingredients.. thus came the existence of chicken chop, chicken grill, lamb chop and bistik (beef steak) with homemade french fries..just like what you see after we gain independence, most of the hainanese chef lost their job and they started to open their own coffee shops (kopitiam) and introduced these western foods to the public.. since then the malaysian style western foods has gone through a lot of changes with the touch of fusions..hopefully all westerners understands the origin of our style of western food. it is called western food not because of our imagination but because of history..

malaysian "western" food is what locals used to serve the english back when malaysia was a colony. contrary to popular belief, a dish like chicken chop isn't what malaysians thought was what westerners ate, it's entirely a malaysian dish created to suit the tastes of the english.my favorite chicken chop place is very close to ss2, it's a stall at the furthest right corner in uptown hawker centre at damansara utama called steven's western corner. damansara areas + ttdi really is the food center in kv. i know everybody has their own preferences, but you'll find the best cuisines of multiple cultures in that like 10² km region. my favorite malay, indian, chinese, american, english, brazilian, middle eastern, and fusion foods are located entirely within the damansaras and ttdi.

define kumquat kumquat is a small, oval-shaped citrus fruit that is typically 2-5 centimeters in length. it is native to southeast asia and is also cultivated in other parts of the world, including the united states, spain, and italy. kumquats have a thin, edible skin and a sweet-tart flavor that is often described as a cross between an orange and a lemon. they are often eaten whole, including the skin, and are used in a variety of culinary applications, including desserts, jams, and cocktails. define lime lime is a small, round citrus fruit that is typically 3-6 centimeters in diameter. it is native to southeast asia and is also cultivated in other parts of the world, including mexico, brazil, and india. limes have a thin, green skin and a tart, acidic flavor that is often used to add brightness and acidity to dishes. they are commonly used in a variety of culinary applications, including drinks, marinades, and desserts. limes are also a rich source of vitamin c and other nutrients.

most clams lack haemoglobin in their blood, the protein molecule that gives our blood it’s colour. blood clams though, do contain haemoglobin. this gives them both their somewhat gruesome appearance when opened, often oozing blood all over the unlucky individual. more importantly though, it makes them susceptible to the same blood diseases as humans, notably hepatitis a, typhoid, and dysentery.not every clam is deadly, and, on the surface, there is nothing wrong with blood clams. that is to say, if raised and harvested correctly, they are harmless. indeed, many attests to their deliciousness and delicate taste. the problem is not with the clams themselves, but the environment they’re raised in.it is only chinese blood clams that are banned in the united states for one very simple reason — sanitation. accurate records of the levels of pollution…

pantai timur's (informally a label for the east coast states i.e. kelantan, terengganu, pahang) cuisine is generally speaking quite unique in its own taste, presentation & ingredients.nasi kerabu, nasi dagang, daging opor/rendang pahang, laksam, otak-otak, akok, keropok lekor are some of the famous ones.no other states in the peninsular, east malaysia or even other closely related region (culturally) like sumatera come close in that respect (uniqueness in taste).i say this as a person who was born in kl, with heritage from northern region, definitely without bias.

Khao yam is not a Malaysian food at all. But Khao Yam is the local food of the people in the three southern border provinces. If it's original, it must be topped with black budu juice. This will be Budu juice that has a sweet taste because it is mixed with sugar. Many Thai people in the three southern border provinces open restaurants there. because it is next to the Thai-Malay border Judging from the accent of the employees in the shop, it is a Yawi dialect from three provinces. if any of you are thinking about visiting kota bharu (kb) for holiday or shopping or whatever.. let me give you a pro tip, don't go there during any of the malaysia public holiday. kelantanese got a culture of working outside kelantan called "merantau". they will usually return to their hometown during the long public holiday. making nearly 1 million cars entering kota bharu in span of 2-3 days during that short period of time. the traffic will be hell. you could be stuck on the road for nearly 4-5 hours. visit kb during any other period of time, you will be fine.

18:44 two classic kelantanese desserts: their names must be inspired by their appearances, one is tahi itek (literally duck poop) and the other jala mas (literally gilded net). both sweet and creamy, used to be favourite desserts of the kelantan royal households and said to originate from the royal cuisine of siam (previous name for thailand). in fact many kelantanese dishes bear similarities to thai dishes - not surprising since until 1909 kelantan was under siamese suzerainty.

26 minute ish, the thing that is called masak lemak is not curry, but masak lemak (it's own thing).malaysians don't call masak lemak as curry. curry is curry, masak lemak is masak lemak.different flavour profile. different ingredients although there are some common ingredients used. different origin as you explained in the video.the main difference between masak lemak and curry is that curry use chilli powder and also curry leaves which masak lemak didn't use. masak lemak use lemon grass which is not used in curry. the spices are also quite different. but i guess foreigner would be more familiar with the term curry more than masak lemak. so using the term curry make it easier for other foreigners to understand.

malaysia and indonesia in ancient times were part of a territorial concept called nusantara (nusa meaning island, and antara meaning the farthest or outermost). the main central kingdoms in nusantara were srivijaya in sumatra in the 8th century, and majapahit in java in the 14th century. the second point is that many ethnic groups from indonesia, between the 14th and 19th centuries, migrated to malaysia and eventually blended with the local malays there. this means it is undeniable that a great deal of indonesian culture has been preserved there, and there is absolutely nothing wrong with that. however, it would be wise for the malaysian side to be a little more thoughtful when presenting their culture, especially concerning its origins. greetings from indonesia.

i'm so glad and surprised that you managed to find sabahan food in kl! i lived and worked in sarawak, near the sabah border so the indigenous people living in the town where i was will have a mix of both sarawakian and sabahan dishes with their rice (which looks exactly the same as yours, only mushier, stickier and completely wrapped in leaves (nuba laya) so that it's easier to transport and eat when they're foraging in the forest). and the dishes are always so unbelievably good. like it's a crime that this cuisine is still little known in peninsular malaysia. the fermented pork (pekasam babi) in particular is so so good. hope you get to try it next time!

simple answer about sabahan and sarawak cuisine is that they have a lot less influenced from indian spices or middle eastern spices and also a lot less of red chilies brought in by portuguese becasue portuguese never colonized them. their food are much more pure and have basic seasonings and they used a lot of black pepper as their main spice. they also used a lot less coconut milk and most of their food is soup based and a lot more jungle greens than commercial greens. their food culture is almost like japanese food culture that emphasis more on the ingrediencies itself rather than the spices and seasonings. its what the malay food would look like if the indians, chinese , portuguese and middle eastern never set foot in our region. in peninsula malaysia you can get this kinda of food but very rare nowadays and you can only get them in remote town in the east coast like in pahang state for example that love a soupy sour based food rather than coconut milk and spices heavy food.

malaysia food doesn't get the respect that thai and viet food gets because it's not as versatile and doesn't compete with the complexity. thai and viet food often feel more complex, with more distinct flavor contrasts inside a dish, while malaysian food often feels more deep, with stronger layering and intensity within a tighter flavor core.this is also going to be a problem internationally where food choices around health are a consideration. thai and viet foods are considerably lower calorie while still packing a ton of flavour. malaysian food by contrast is far too calorically dense. this is important for countries that have a more health conscious population. just look at the obesity rates:- malaysia: 19.7% (between 34 - 36 million) - thailand: 11.6% (nearing 72 million) - vietnam: 1.7% - 2.1% (sources vary)(over 102 million)malaysia's population is near top 10 most obese in asia, while thailand (getting closer to malaysia) and vietnam have lower rates. considering also, the population of these 3 sea countries, malaysia has a significant problem being the country with the smallest population.

chinese food, malay food, indian food, peranakan food. throw in thai influences, indonesian influences, arabic, northern chinese, muslim chinese, etc. even amongst chinese, there are ethnic differences. hokkien, cantonese, teochew, hakka, hainan. there are also geographical differences. go north the hokkien mee is soup. central in kl, it is fried almost dry in black sauce. south its mee with bee hoon fried dry without black sauce. in kl and klang, bak kut teh is cooked in soya sauce. in the south its clear soup like with plenty of garlic. ask a johorean, there is only one style of mee rebus, not the “fake” you get up north. ask the johorean, wonton mee is in dry chili sauce. the kl born person will disagree and say the soya sauce drenched wet version is the way to go. stay and travel long enough, you will start to appreciate the differences.

this ikan bakar place is quite viral and there are actually 4 different stalls here. i’ve tried all of them, and honestly most of the flavours are quite similar — some even a bit sweet and slightly bland.but the one that really stood out to me is ikan bakar seri melaka. funny enough, it’s actually the smallest stall and the one with the least crowd. i think most people tend to go to the bigger, more “gah” looking restaurants.for me, seri melaka is the best. the fish tastes fresh, the rempah feels more like authentic kampung-style ikan bakar, and the kuah asam is just perfect — pedas, masam, and really cukup rasa.what i like the most is the way they serve the food. you can really feel the sincerity, the staff are friendly, and the service is top. honestly they deserve more support, so if you’re coming here please give ikan bakar seri melaka a try. small stall, but really big on flavour.

59:01 the real origin of teh tarik is hot tea water then mixed with fresh cow's milk, it's the same as in india.. when this tea water dish was introduced to the malay community, the malays didn't like to drink hot and bubbling drinks, they would pour a little bit on a small plate, let it cool a little, then drink it, over time it changed by drawing the hot water until it foamed, then it became teh tarik, while fresh milk was replaced with condensed milk because it was difficult to get fresh milk and many water makers realized that condensed milk made teh tarik water more foamy and many people were interested in it

in malay, i pronounce 'mee low' & english, it's 'my low' 😁😁.. as for the laksa it's curry, you're right. the singaporean call their curry noodle 'laksa' which is with coconut milk with fish cakes/balls, prawns, chicken flesh & veggies. malaysian version comes with cockles, chicken shreds/prawns/ fish cakes or balls in our curry noodle. another laksa version is asam laksa (asam = tamarind). it's fish flesh & broth. it's sourish, sweet & spicy with lots of cucumber, onion, pineapple & mint leaves. these days the sellers are adding the fish cakes/balls to the asam laksa which to me is not original at all.

bruv, malaysian here. fyi tuk din means grandpa din..din being owner's name i presume. i think you triggered every malaysian eating nasi lemak (fatty rice). the rice is cooked with coconut milk. here in malaysia we use fresh coconut milk, not from a can. the hot sauce, called sambal, is key for this dish. it needs to be sweet, savoury and spicy with a fishy taste, as it usually has ground anchovies in it. and you need to use a lot more per spoonful. people mix it til most of the rice turns red here in malaysia. spicy level ranges from a 1 to 10. and as other people have mentioned, it all goes in the mouth at once! even the curry noodle you ate. fork some noodle, every protein and veggie you can find, and stuff your mouth. chase this with 2-3 spoonfuls of curry each time. now repeat til finish. i nearly switched off watching you eat these 2 favourites of mine. lol.hope you get to try other malaysian dishes sometime.

28:04 folding the leaf has two way you fold down (you're satisfied with the meal and the service) fold up (you're either showing dissatisfaction either with meal or how it's served. (this changes for certain regions the opposite way but for the most this is the way ) and fun fact people would hold feasts called moi virundhu. moi is a money offering more like when people in japan give money during new years. but moi is given either in weddings funerals or in some cases in feasts. these are held especially when someone is in need of money. either for a good thing or a bad thing doesn't matter. they announce it to the whole locality and people would eat then give the money offering under their leaf or in the hands. ( some moi feasts that are usually held annually gatehr upto literally millions of dollars) . and there's a buisness model based on this too. where you gather eat and give the money. but not in restaurant but in halls in a certain day.

it's "banjir" (flood), not banjur. overcrowding your plate when having a nasi kandar is common. the next time just ask them for a 2nd plate so you can move some stuff over.as for the cendol, there are other palm sugars across malaysia, but all vendors "generally" use gula melaka nationwide.there are 2 main chili pan mee competitors - kin kin & super kitchen. i say kin kin is slightly sweeter & super kitchen is slightly salty/savoury. i'm a super kitchen fan, so i'd recommend you try that the next time you're around. also, i've said the same damn thing a 100 times before, they should bottle up & sell that chili oil.yup, cincau is black glass jelly. btw, "gula" means sugar. so generally speaking, if you just say "gula" to a malaysian, they'll assume you're referring to white cane sugar.as for coins, we call it "cents" too. for example, 1 ringgit 80 cents. the malay spelling is "sen."

as a penang chinese guy, three dishes that are on top of everything, they are keow teow teng (pork soup keow teow), wantan mee (pork mince wantan with egg noodle), and the last one is char keow teow.they are the three kings.then later there are hokkien mee (prawn soup noodle), asam laksa and curry noodle, they are the three dukes.the the less famous but also delicious such as keow chap (pork, duck, chicken based soup noodles), jawa mee (tomato soup noodle), char tua pan (wok fried big sizes noodle served with gravy soup, pork and prawn based food). these are the three counts. the remaining rest as above than average food from chinese.as for malays and indians food, they are colourful and coconut milk based, and very tasty.so, after all delicious penang food, maybe your body become so hot, you can drink green coconut water too. ahha

i feel like the nasi lemak at the start is not just good cooking but more importantly genius packaging/marketing, by stacking the one side dish on top of the rice, wrapped beautifully in a banana leaf with sambal slathered on it. something tells me it’s not that special in terms of flavour. in most places that would be insufficient for nasi lemak, esp with just ikan bilis, egg, sambal, rice, but they made it look like a good quantity with genius packaging. in other places, the nasi lemak, sambal, and side dishes are served separately, so you can easily tell when the ingredients are few.my fav nasi lemak place has rice, sambal, eggs, chicken, prawns, and otah. some say it’s too much but not necessarily if the chef knows how to complement everything together. and no, the side dishes are all served separately (on the same plate but not stacked into a ‘ball’ like the video’s one) because the side dishes are tasty enough to eat on their own.

you’ve got the origins of chicken rice a bit mixed up. it was the hainanese migrants who brought the dish from hainan to singapore, malaysia, and other places. they arrived later than other chinese groups, such as those from quanzhou, chaoshan, and other coastal areas. by the time the hainanese came, the coolie trade was already saturated, and opportunities in commerce—like the tea and chinese goods trade—were limited. as a result, many of them found work as cooks for the british colonial households. this eventually led them to venture into the food and beverage industry.their entry into this space gave rise to several uniquely hainanese culinary creations that are distinct to singapore and malaysia. among them was their localized version of wenchang chicken, adapted using local poultry and ingredients. naturally, the dish evolved differently in various regions due to the availability of ingredients and local preferences.

blondie .. do keep these three upcoming dates in your calendar. it will be months of shopping, celebration, culture and delicious foods .. (1) christmas 25/12 (2) chinese new year & starting of ramadhan -17 feb . besides chinese new year celebration , we have ramadhan food bazaar in the afternoon, you may also join the crowd to break fast with the locals at merdeka square, putra jaya or at mosques for the whole one month. come and experience it (3) eid fitri 20 march. one whole month of another food from family and friends gathering. you will find all kind of malay foods. some you will not find in any other month eg lemang, ketupat and rendang. these 3 months will also be month of shopping for many. so bring along your dad. malaysia will give you 3 months visa to enjoy them all.
